speaker: interviewer
text: Wells interview, tape two. Dr. Wells, we were just starting to discuss your education in the gymnasium. Now, that was your secular education. Did you also continue with your Jewish education at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely, yes. Every day, I-- in Poland, in gymnasium, Jewish or non-Jewish, you had to have a uniform with a number on your arm. So they knew where you belong, and so on, in case of any-- because some students made trouble in the night, and [INAUDIBLE]. But the Yeshiva, or the Jewish school, did not allow you to go to gymnasium. The authorities did not allow you. So I had to have with me, two hats in my briefcase, one for the morning, to go to school, and after school, I went to Yeshiva. I took it off and put on the other hat. So it was a normal procedure. It was no problem, and no big deal about it. And so I went to Yeshiva in the afternoon, and in the morning to gymnasium. You know, gymnasium was till 1 or 2 o'clock, And after 2 o'clock, by 3 o'clock, I was in Yeshiva until the evening. I was at the Yeshiva, learning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was life for the Jews in 1938 in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- as far as I know, I did not think about anything. They used to tell me, and we knew about it, they are at certain streets in the night, some fighting between the Jews and their kids, right wing. And the right wing, with the fights, so again, the number helped, because every school said, don't go out in the night in these-- these streets. And if we catch you, we will throw you out of school. So-- and for me, I never went there in any case, in these streets. And so it wasn't at any time of any problem between '38 and '39. And-- and you know, so it was no--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear about Kristallnacht, in November of '38? What did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I heard about the Kristallnacht in Germany. And it was because of a hooligan in-- in Paris that killed this German diplomat, Grynszpan. And he killed the German diplomat. And the Kristallnacht had, for us Jews, a showing that Germany is still a cultured country. Namely, that if it would have been a pogrom in Russia, or Poland, such a pogrom would have been killing people. Here, they broke windows and they looted stores. The Jews had to clean certain streets with the brushes, or not, what they should. But that was no big deal for us. But they did not have a pogrom, killing women and children and houses, going in to kill. I believe the whole Kristallnacht, a few people were killed. No big-- in proportion, what we considered no big deal. And so a [? choate, ?] you see, these were the [? choate. ?] That if you organize, and if you have a cultured people, they don't do anything out of line. Everything is organized. And as long as it's organized, it's OK. So it wasn't positive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a [? choate, ?] that the Germans still behave properly, and for us, in the other community, there was these Germans Jews that were always assimilated. They want to be assimilated. They want to be Germans. Good for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the God punishment on them, not on us. So it has two, in two, three, you can go on and on with this philosophy. So I remember in the shtiebel in the synagogue, we sat at the table. They told what's happening. And nothing of any serious, except that the Germans are cultured people. That was the Kristallnacht. And this is why I cannot speak about it, in spite that I know what it means politically today, and so on. But I cannot speak about, because for us it was exactly the opposite of fact. What we feel today, we want to make a fact of Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then when did things begin to change for you in Lvov?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, it became a change in 1939, when the Russians came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Russians came in, they took away the privacy of the people. It was a non-religious group. It was most of the Jews who were for it, but the very religious Jews where religion, it was number one. And here it was anti-religious, and so on. But as my father would say, if you don't get involved in any politics, nobody ever touches you. Number one, which was true. But number two, it was a negative for us, because my father had the apartment house. And so when they took these bourgeois, the rich people, bourgeois, to Siberia, Russia took them out-- took them away in the night. We were hiding in the basement for these two, three nights. The thing was over. They did not take any more. You got out. And my father had a job with them finished. So you had to wait for the next to go. But for me, as a child, it was no big problem. I even applied to a university in Moscow, to go to university to study. Because it was a first time spoken about universities, and so on. Because until this time, I never thought about it. But you know, going higher in the gymnasium, you started thinking they were free. And people talked to you. And I was a good student. I was one the 10 chosen in my gymnasium that I can get a scholarship, that they will educate me more. And so I went even to apply for it. So it wasn't in any way, a very deep consequences or tragedy, except the family wasn't anymore as a unity of this orthodox community.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents feel about the prospect of you going to Russia to study, and how did they feel about the challenges to your religious faith?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did not really-- they had already ambiguous feelings themselves. But till a certain [INAUDIBLE], they were religious. But I don't believe they really-- it was a different world, and so on. You know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because we were brought up with the idea of God, if I look at it differently today, than at this time. But the idea of God, and so on, very often you feel it if it is a problem. I believe in God, and he will take care of it. It wasn't always your problem, if you believe in it. And if you don't believe in, so what's the big deal? You know, it's-- So when were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you accepted with the scholarship into the university?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the end of '41. And you know, I applied, it was June, to start September. And at this time during the summer, I will get all the papers and everything. And the war broke out in June, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember I was in the park. I did not even think about anything. You have now the summer, and after the summer, we'll continue. You know, you don't get right overnight all the answers, and I didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had even a little bit, I remember something that I had an answer. Because I-- my papers, some of the papers of-- that I was accepted, I found after the war, in Moscow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it must have been that I sent it out, or the school send it out. You know, you're that-- you are 16 years old, 15, and if you don't think in the terms of what-- you know-- you don't even know what you really want to do. You know? My kids were in the third year, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] school, college, university. You couldn't ask her, what do you really want to do at the year. So do you want? You know? It will be. Yet, I knew I would study accounting or mathematics or physics, and that's all to it. You did not think in other terms. So then what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war broke out. And within one week from the war, they arrested 5,000 people, and I was among these 5,000. I and my father. And was taken to Czwartakow in Lvov.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name, it was Czwartakow, C-Z-W-A-R-T-A-K-O-W. And I was taken--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a street, or an area?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in an-- a street, in a center for the military center.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And to you speak about it, how people lie to themselves, what's really happening. We tried to lie to our friends, what really happened. For example, I was with a friend's father. And afterwards, I said, no your father wasn't there, to him, to my friend, in the same street. And they took all the people, and shot them, the 5,000 people, near Lvov. Near town of Lvov. And I-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you laid overnight, straight light on your face all night. In the morning, when the trucks came to pick you up, and they took 40 people at a time to be shot near Lvov. We did not know what for it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Called, who wants to go to work? And I wanted to go right away. My father laid next to me. He said, don't go by yourself. If they call you, you go. If they don't call you, don't be-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] don't go. Don't volunteer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't be a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to go, and so on. And I-- so I did not go. That it was. And by Friday night, and that was Tuesday night, then-- Wednesday night, Thursday night, and Friday night. By Friday night, there were left about 200, 300 people. They did not ship it. They had the Saturday was the free day in Germany. In Poland, we worked, but in Germany, they had a free day. So they shipped us home by Friday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you been fed in between?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Oh, yeah, very-- nothing. Practically nothing two days you can survive without feeding. And this is why I wanted to go, because I wasn't fed. I was thirsty. You lay on your face, you pee under yourself here. Do everything. You don't move for 48 hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is why, when every time they called, I said, I'm going! And my father said, no, you lay. So until they don't call you by the name, you don't go. So you leave it to the action, but you don't do anything. So you, for these 40, this is why you wanted to be-- to go by yourself. And then what happened when you were released?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a different time than today, because by Friday night, my mother's seeing, finally gathered we are in Czwartakow, went even after the hours. After 8 o'clock, you couldn't go out the street for anybody. You know, it was wartime. And she went there, because on that idea, they will not touch a woman. Nobody's going to touch a woman. So she went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I came out, when they set release, I came out and saw my mother standing outside. And I said, how did you get here? I was trying to get you out! You know, but nobody thought at these times that they will touch women and children. And I was 16. But I was not anymore a child, not considered to be a young person. So and I came. Who were the 5,000 people that they took and shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Lvov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they just men at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men. Men.

speaker: interviewer
text: What ages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men. I don't know if it was 45, 50. Ages from 16 to 45 or 50.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they left the younger children? They left them alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they did not touch young children and the women.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did anybody have any idea-- when you were released, did they realize that these people had all been shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no. We believed in it. Like, I, am laying there for 48 hours, started to believe that they may be. But the reality, we thought, about that they are taken to work. Because I even-- because afterwards, were rumors that somebody saw them near Vienna. They were taken to Germany to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we, coming out from there, supported this idea. Oh, yeah, they were taken to Vienna. Because how can you tell to your friends-- to your friend in the same state, no, I saw your father. He was taken to be shot. I did not see it. And how can you say, no, he was taken to be shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they took them to Vienna to work. Oh, yes. Yes, I know it. If somebody said, I even saw them on the train to Vienna. You did not say no, they were taken by trucks. You said they were. You did not say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as I say, the idea of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there are some rumors going around, or that we lied, we did it ourselves. They needed only to give a general idea, and finished. It spread like fire if it was positive. If it were-- if they would have said they were taken to be shot, if my friend came-- he was 16 years old-- and said, my father was shot, oh, no, no. And I did not see it. But you said, no, no. They were-- they-- you know, you helped the idea of propaganda or anything. And you say, how did they believe? You yourself wanted to believe positive things. So you do it. You don't-- So what happened then? It was the end of June '41.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was quiet for a while. And then a month later, was again, what do you call it? Petliura. It's the anniversary of Petliura. It is a Ukrainian leader, national leader. It was a pogrom. They beat up and on. And they caught me again. I was in the basement from this time on. In the morning, I went down in hiding. And again, here, my father being about 47, 48, '94 and '41, 6, 47, was doing one thing. He was laying in bed, sick. They wouldn't touch him either. They would go and find him sick. Finished. But I couldn't play sick. You know, I was too young. And so-- so I was hiding in the basement. But I was discovered this morning. And I was taken to-- on the railroad. And I was beaten and so on. But they released us in the night. This time it wasn't yet taken to be shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, where did they take you on the railroad? Where were you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lvov, in the railroad, working on the-- you know-- beating, beating work. But on Saturdays, I kept hiding and hiding, until about a month later, I got myself a job on the railroad, and this job gave me guarantee that they will not take me. I had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I could show that I'm working on an important military installation. And I went every morning to work. In the night, I came home late, so that they cannot catch me again. So I normally came back after sunset, 9 o'clock, 8:00, 9 o'clock, from the railroad. I worked all day, and I went right to sleep. And in the morning, at 5:30, I got up before they started to take people out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, as I said, he went with me to work on the railroad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. But before this he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was playing sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: --sick. He wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your other brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were too young.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were safe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kids were safer. Safe, safer. Safe, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there enough to eat in Lvov at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. It was very tight. You know, you got-- but you got like a certain amount of bread a day, and you had to make do. You know, it was hunger, but it wasn't starving of hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did this work on the railroad continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: It continued till end of February, beginning of March.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1942. March 1942. My father did not go everyday to work. Certain days he laid at home. And I went everyday at 4 o'clock in the morning till 9:00. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in 1942, it was a Saturday, they called out names, people that don't come to work everyday. You know, that don't come to work. And I said to my father, you don't have to go. If they call your name, I will be there. Because-- and if it's something unpleasant, something bad, I will show you, I am Leon Wells, so they got the bad-- the wrong name. Because I am a good worker. And I'm everyday, I did not miss one day of work, since September or October, I don't even remember. So fine. But they came on Saturday, and they took us all by truck. They called out the name, and I came. And I said, I am Wells. Weliczker.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there, and they took me to Lvov. To the center. to the headquarters. And I came to the headquarters, And they all took us to the Janowska camp. I wanted to backtrack for one moment before we go on with Janowska. In this time, up to 1942, were you able to be practicing Jews? Were you able to go for services? How restricted was that part of your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we did not go. We did not think about going to services. We did not go out. And that was about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was the end of Jewish observance at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, it was. I remember, it was the Yom Kippur before, in '41. They had in the shtiebel, they got together a few people. But it wasn't anything of any importance.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you weren't in great danger by doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't great danger, but nobody thought in terms of greater danger or great-- you-- you do certain things without even thinking. I don't know. But maybe because I was young, maybe they thought, or not, I cannot. But I myself was, this is how it is. This is how it is. The whole idea was that the people of my father's age, being from World War I, knew that if England, France, and Russia's going to war with Germany, the war will be over in no time. And when it started, and it wasn't over, they had a very simple calculation.

speaker: interviewer
text: World War I lasted four years. Today, with airplanes, and on, with bombing, in few months it will be over, one way or another. So it wasn't anything. You had to survive the day. It wasn't that you have to survive for how many years, or what will happen? If you survive the day, by tomorrow, it could be at the end. Which-- and when England said, we will be ready for the war in '44, and my father was reading. And I remember meeting with some people, everybody was laughing. How can a war with airplanes last till 1944? It will over in no time.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it is joking. They probably joked. They did not mean it, or they don't know even what's going on. My father and his friends knew more than England or America what was going on. They don't even know what they are talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now it's March of '42. Tell me about--

speaker: interviewee
text: In March, 2, '42, I got into the Janowska camp. This is how I got in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they transport you? You said in trucks?

speaker: interviewee
text: In trucks. They took us over there, and finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was that from the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in town. It was like saying, Washington Heights from Lower East Side, how far is it? You know, it was a town. Janowska was a street in Lvov. It was on the outskirts, but it was in the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain to me the process by which you were taken in. And how many people were there in the group that was taken with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't a big group, maybe 40, 50 people that did not come to work. You know, that-- how many, most of it. Of us went every day, the same as I. Everyday from morning till evening, and we worked. There wasn't in any way you even thought not to go to work. And once we got in, they took us in, shaved us. We were at the baths. And got into the-- a number, and that was all. And I remember like today, we were registered. And there was a woman, a Jewish woman, a secretary, who worked also in the camp. But she worked there. And she said to me, you're a young boy. You will not survive here 24 hours. Don't you have some kind of a profession that we can put you in in some kind of a professional place, so that you are not in this state and not being beaten every day, and so on. And I worked until the last-- in a month, months, I worked in-- near-- across our house, in this month that I was hiding during the day-- in a chocolate factory, [INAUDIBLE]. And I worked as an assistant to a glazier. He had to put in glass in all the windows, because they were bombed out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said-- the first thing I said, I am in wood, lumber. And she said, lumber's no good. You will be outside all day. You are out to be beaten every day. So I said, how about a glazier? She said, that's a good profession, because a glazier's inside, and it will be warmer for you. And this is how I became as a glazier.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only trouble was when I said a glazier, and she said, good. I was the only one glazier. I thought of being an assistant to a glazier, somebody will do the work, and I will help him out. But I did not know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't-- we'll stop right here and continue in a moment.
